coredata.dc:title: Blue-LED-excited Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide luminescent nanocrystals for selective and sensitive Fe3+ ions sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Bingxin
Lei, Lei
Liu, Enyang
Bai, Gongxun
Ye, Renguang
Xu, Shiqing
coredata.dc:description: Novel blue LED excitable Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide nanocrystals are exploited for selective and sensitive Fe3+ ions sensing in this work. The detection limit is calculated to be as low as 92.5 nM, which is better than most previous ultra-violet light excited fluorescence probes. The decreased radiative probability of the Ce3+: 5d by the NC surface environmental variation activates the sensing capability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Blue LED
Photoluminescent
Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide
Fe3+ ions sensing

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: PL measurements of the NCs suspension with different metal ions

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
Natural Science Foundation of Zhejiang Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CaS (a), Ca0·5Sr0·5S (b) and SrS (c), Schematic illustration for the synthesis of alkaline-earth sulfide NCs.

(a) PL emission spectra of the CaS: xCe: (x = 0.01, 0.04, 0.07, 0.5, 1) NCs under the excitation of 450 nm. PL emission spectra (b) and PL excitation spectra of Ca(1-y)SryS: 0.07Ce: (y = 0, 0.5, 0.7, 1). (d) PL decay curves for the 475 nm and 530 nm of the SrS: Ce NCs. (e) Dgital photograghs of the CaS: xCe: (x = 0.01, 0.04, 0.07, 0.5, 1) NCs suspensions.

(a) PL intensity of the SrS: Ce NCs suspension with various concentration of Fe3+ ions under the excitation of 430 nm blue LED, (b) Integral luminescent intensity variations versus Fe3+ ions concentration. (c) The variation of (I0/I) - 1 versus Fe3+ ions concentration. (d) PL intensities of SrS: Ce NCs suspensions with the addition of different metal ions.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the Fe 2p (a) and S 2p (b). (c) Schematic illustration for the proposed Fe3+ ions sensing mechanism.
Integral PL intensities of the Ce3+ ions of various suspensions, which are normalized by the blank SrS: Ce NCs suspensions.

head.ce:title: Blue-LED-excited Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide luminescent nanocrystals for selective and sensitive Fe3+ ions sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou, 310018, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Optoelectronic Materials and Devices

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Optoelectronic Materials and Devices, China Jiliang University, Hangzhou, 310018, People's Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel blue LED excitable Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide nanocrystals are exploited for selective and sensitive Fe3+ ions sensing in this work. The detection limit is calculated to be as low as 92.5 nM, which is better than most previous ultra-violet light excited fluorescence probes. The decreased radiative probability of the Ce3+: 5d by the NC surface environmental variation activates the sensing capability.

A new PL sensing route for Fe3+ ions is introduced based on the blue LED excitable Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide NCs. The detection limit is as low as 92.5 nM.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Trivalent iron ion (Fe3+) is the most abundant trace metal ion in human body and plays an important role in multiple physiological processes, such as DNA replication [1–6], cellular life cycles [7,8] and hemoglobin formation [9–11]. Excess Fe3+ content in living systems can cause cardiac, hepatic or diabetes diseases [12–17], while deficient amount of Fe3+ can lead to anemia diseases, decreased immunity and human neurological disorders [18–21]. In addition, the widespread application of Fe3+ ions in industry also causes Fe3+-contained wastes and serious environmental pollution [22–24]. In this scenario, several efforts have been devoted to develop sensitive and selective strategies for Fe3+ ions sensing. Compared with traditional methods including atomic absorption spectrometry, inductively plasma mass spectroscopy, electrochemical sensors, colorimetry and UV–vis spectrophotometry, photoluminescent (PL) sensing strategy possesses several excellent advantages, such as low-cost, non-invasive, high sensitivity, fast response and remote operation [25–29]. As a result, it is highly significant and valuable to explore novel PL probes with superior performances.

A series of PL probes, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [30–32], carbon-involved quantum dots [33–35], and lanthanide doped PL probes [36,37] have been broadly studied for Fe3+ ions sensing, however, they are harmful to the human cells and suffer from strong background fluorescence owing to the requirement of ultra-violet (UV) excitation source. Although a few organics and semiconductor quantum dots that excited under blue or green light have been investigated for the determination of Fe3+ ions [38–41], they exhibit the disadvantages of high biotoxicity, low contrast in sensing signal or poor photochemical stability. Lanthanide ions possess abundant energy levels and the corresponding effective absorption wavelengths expand from UV to near infrared. Especially, the electric-dipole allowed 4f-5d transition of trivalent cerium ion (Ce3+) in the blue region has large absorption cross-section and can be tuned to match with the emission of a GaN blue LED chip [42,43]. Moreover, the electrons on 5d orbital of the Ce3+ ion are very sensitive to the local crystal environment, which has been used for pH sensing [44]. In this scenario, it is reasonable to predict that Ce3+-doped inorganic NCs may be suitable for important metal ions sensing under the excitation of blue LED.

As a proof-of-concept, Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide NCs with different shapes and sizes are prepared. Under the excitation of blue light, the Ce3+ ions output strong green emission. With the substitution of Ca2+ ions by Sr2+ ions, the Ce3+ emission wavelength exhibits blue-shift, which is owing to the reduced crystal field splitting followed by the higher location of the Ce3+: 5d level. More interestingly, the PL intensity of the Ce3+ ions decreases greatly with the addition of Fe3+ ions into the Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide NCs suspension, indicating the present studied NCs are much appropriate for the determination of Fe3+ ions. The detection limit is measured to be ~92.5 nM, which is better than most previous Fe3+ ions sensing probes (Table S1). Our results also verify the present studied Fe3+ ions sensing probes possess highly selectivity.

(C2H3O2)2Ca·H2O (99.9%) was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (C2H3O2)2Sr was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. (C2H3O2)3Ce·4H2O (99.99%), C2H3O2Li (99.99%), C2H3O2Na (99.995%), Oleic acid (OA), oleylamine (OM), Trioctylamine (TOA), and N,N′-diphenylthiourea(DPTU) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Cyclohexane, chloroform, acetone, and ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China. Distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

FeCl3·6H2O (99%), KCl (99.99%), LiCl (99.99%), C2H3O2Na (99.995%), (C2H3O2)2Ba (99.99%), (C2H3O2)2Cu·H2O (99%), were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). (C2H3O2)3Y·xH2O (99.99%), (C2H3O2)3Gd·xH2O (99.9%), (C2H3O2)2Zn (99%), (C2H3O2)3Mn·2H2O (97%) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. (C2H3O2)2Ca·H2O (99.9%) was purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. TiF4 (99%), (C2H3O2)2Mn·4H2O (98%) were purchased from J&K Scientific Ltd. AlCl3·3H2O (99%) was purchased from Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co. Ltd. Cr(NO3)3·xH2O (99.9%), Cyclohexane (AR), ethanol (AR) were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company. Doubly distilled water was used throughout the experiments.

The synthetic procedure for the monodisperse CaxSr1-xS: Ce3+ nanoparticles (NPs) were similar to the previous literatures reported by Zhang et al. [45,46] Taking CaS:0.07Ce as an example, (CH3COO)2Ca·H2O (0.9993 mmol), 70 μL aqueous solution of (CH3COO)3Ce·4H2O (0.01 M), 2 mL OA, 12 mL OM and 6 mL TOA were added in a 50 mL three-neck flask at room temperature (RT). The mixture was heated to 120 °C under a high purified nitrogen atmosphere for 30 min to remove water and other impurities with low boiling points, and then heated to 160 °C and stirred for another 30 min to form a clear solution. After cooling down to room temperature (RT), 10 mL of ethanol solution containing 3 mmol of DPTU was added and the solution was stirred at 90 °C for 1h to remove ethanol. After ethanol was evaporated, the resulting solution was heated to 320 °C and maintained for 1h under a high purified nitrogen atmosphere and then cooled down to RT. The products were precipitated with absolute ethanol, collected by centrifugation, washed with cyclohexane and ethanol for three times, and finally redispersed in cyclohexane. SrS was prepared by a similar method, except that (CH3COO)2Ca·H2O was substituted by (C2H3O2)2Sr. CaS: Li and CaS: Na were prepared by adding extra C2H3O2Li and C2H3O2Na.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PL measurements of the NCs suspension with different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: A series of CaS: Ce3+ cyclohexane suspension with a same concentration were prepared firstly. Then the tested metal salts with designed concentrations were added into those above suspensions separately. It should be noted that the final volume of those metal ions involved NCs suspension was kept the same to make sure the PL intensity variations were only attributed to influence of the metal ions. Ethanol was used to dissolve those metal salts which could not be dissolved well in cyclohexane, and the same content of ethanol was added into the blank CaS: Ce3+ cyclohexane suspension as well.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were recorded by Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffraction using Cu-Kα (1.5405 Å) radiation. The morphologies of the products were studied using an FEI Tecnai G2 F20 S-WTINE transmission electron microscope (operated at 200 kV). Energy dispersive X-ray spectroscope (EDS)-mapping analysis was performed by JEM ARM200F at 200 kV. TEM specimens were prepared by directly drying a drop of a dilute cyclohexane dispersion solution of the products on the surface of a carbon coated copper grid. Photoluminescence properties were recorded on a PL3-211-P spectrometer (HORIBA JOBIN YVON, America) assembled with xenon lamp (450 W). Life-time decay curves were tested by a fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (FL3-211) at 278 nm ps laser excitation using time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was carried out using a 250Xi ESCA system under Al Ka irradiation.

As shown in Fig. S1a, all the as-prepared Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide luminescent NCs including CaS, Ca0·5Sr0·5S and SrS belong to pure cubic phase. With the substitution of Ca2+ ions (r = 0.1 nm) with Sr2+ ions (r = 0.118 nm) [47], the XRD peaks shift to low angles owing to the expansion of crystal lattice. Ca2+/Sr2+ cations form a face-centered cubic lattice structure, while the S2− anions coordinate with six Ca2+/Sr2+ cations and locate in the octahedral holes (Fig. S1b-d). TEM images reveal that the CaS, Ca0·5Sr0·5S and SrS NCs exhibit column, rhombus and hexagonal shapes, whose mean particle sizes respectively are ~21 nm, ~17 nm and ~55 nm (Fig. 1 a–c). Furthermore, we verify that the incorporation of Li+ ions or Na+ ions can promote the growth of CaS NCs followed by larger particle sizes (Fig. S2), which may due to the incorporation of Li+/Na+ can accelerate the attraction of S2− ions from solution to NC nuclei to make sure the charge balance [48]. The schematic illustration of the synthetic processes is shown in Fig. 1d.

As shown in Fig. 2 a, under the excitation of 450 nm blue light, two broad PL emission bands centered at 500 nm and 560 nm are observed, corresponding to the electronic transitions from T2g (5d state) level to 2F5/2 and 2F7/2 (4f state) levels of Ce3+ ions, respectively. With monitoring the PL emission band at 500 nm, two excitation bands at 280 nm and 450 nm are detected (Fig. S3), which can be assigned to the host absorption and Ce3+: 4f → 5d transition, respectively. The optimal Ce3+ ions doping concentration is ~0.07 mol% (Fig. 2a). Both of the excitation and emission wavelengths for the Ce3+ ions exhibit blue-shift with replacing Ca2+ ions with Sr2+ ions gradually (Fig. 2b and c), which is further evidenced by their corresponding digital photographs (Fig. 2e). Both of the life-times for the 475 nm and 530 nm of the SrS: Ce NCs are measured to be about 9.1 ns (Fig. 2d), indicating these two different emission wavelengths are indeed originated from a same excited energy level.

The blue-shift phenomenon of the Ce3+ ions can be explained by the crystal field theory [49,50], which is expressed by the following equations:
(1)
Δ = 10 D q
(2)
D =
35 Z e
4
R 5
(3)
q =
2 e
105
r 4
Where Δ is a measure of crystal field which represents the crystal-splitting between eg and t2g levels of a d-electron. Z is the anion charge, R is the bond length, e is the electron charge and r is the radius of the d wavefunction. On this occasion, the crystal field splitting is proportional to 1/R5. When part of Ca2+ ions are substituted by Sr2+ ions with larger ionic radius, the bond length of R becomes longer, which leads to the smaller crystal field splitting followed by the higher location of the Ce3+: 5d level [51]. Therefore, the location of the Ce3+: 5d energy level in the SrS NCs is higher than that of CaS NCs, and the emission wavelength is blue-shifted from 500 to 475 nm. In addition, the band gap of SrS is generally smaller than that of CaS, leading to the red-shift of the host absorption band (Fig. 2c) [52]. The schematic illustration for the above blue-shift phenomenon is shown in Fig. S4.

Interestingly, the PL intensity of the SrS: Ce NCs suspension decreases greatly with the addition of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 3 a). Specifically, under the excitation of ~430 nm blue LED, the PL intensity of the Ce3+ ions decreases about 71.8% at the Fe3+ ions concentration of 8 μmol/L (Fig. 3b). Based on the Stern-Volmer equation, the (I0/I) −1 can be fitted well with the Fe3+ ions concentration, where I0 and I represent the PL intensity before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions, respectively. It should be noted that CaS: Ce and Ca0·5Sr0·5S: Ce have similar Fe3+ ions sensing performance with SrS: Ce. On this occasion, the detection limit (DL) is calculated to be 92.5 nM according to the equation of D = 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank measurement, and K is the fitted slope (Fig. 3c). This ultra-high sensitivity is better than most reported fluorescence probes (Table S1). More importantly, a series of other metal ions have little effect on the PL intensity of Ce3+ ions (Fig. 3d and Fig. S8), indicating the present studied Ce3+ ions doped alkaline-earth sulfide NCs is highly suitable for the selectively Fe3+ ions sensing.

To clarify the above Fe3+ ions sensing mechanism, the valence state variations of the Fe3+ and S2− ions on the NC surface were characterized and analyzed. For the pure FeCl3 powder, the high-resolution XPS spectrum of the Fe 2p includes two peaks of 711.7 and 725.4 eV (Fig. S5a). For the SrS:0.07Ce powder, there is only one peak of 161.8 eV corresponding to S2− (Fig. S5b). After the addition of FeCl3 into the SrS: Ce NCs suspension, the two peaks at 710.1 eV and 723.4 eV are assigned to the divalent Fe2+ ions, and the other two peaks are attributed to the trivalent Fe3+ ions (Fig. 4 a). The high-resolution XPS S 2p spectrum (Fig. 4b) shows two separate peaks of 161.5 eV, 163.2 eV and 167.4 eV, which could be assigned to the S2− and SO3 2−, respectively. This dramatically changed NC surface environment could greatly influence the radiative transition probability of the susceptible Ce3+: 5d level. It should be mentioned that NC has large surface-to-volume ratio compared to that of bulk. As shown in Fig. S6, the life-time of Ce3+: 5d decreases from 9.1 ns to 5.4 ns with the addition of 8 μM Fe3+ ions. In addition, the standard electrode potential of Ce4+/Ce3+ (1.70 V) is much higher than that of Fe3+/Fe2+ (0.77 V), indicating the Ce4+ has stronger oxidizing ability than Fe3+ and the Ce3+ is hard to be oxidized by Fe3+. Furthermore, with the addition of same Fe3+ ions content into the hydrochloric and hydrophobic CaS:Ce NCs suspension, the PL intensity of the hydrochloric CaS:Ce NCs is only slightly decreased, while that of hydrophobic CaS:Ce NCs is greatly reduced (Fig. S7), which further reveal that the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+ is highly related to the surface ligand. Based on the above analysis, a possible PL intensity quenching mechanism for the Ce3+ ions is proposed: with the addition of Fe3+ ions into the CaS: Ce NCs suspension, part of the trivalent Fe3+ ions transform into divalent Fe2+ ions, at the same time, part of S2− ions on the NC surface together with OA ligand change to SO3 2−, which greatly influences the crystal environment of the surface Ce3+ ions and reduces the electronic radiative probability of the Ce3+: 5d level followed by the decreased PL intensity. The schematic illustration for the above Fe3+ ions sensing mechanism is shown in Fig. 4c.

We finally show the present studied SrS: Ce NCs have potential application for Fe3+ ions determination in vegetable oils. Firstly, Fe3+ ions concentration of 0.5, 1, 2, 3 μM in respectively commercial corn oil, peanut oil, colza oil and soybean oil were achieved by the addition of certain amount of FeCl3 into those vegetable oils. Then a drop of the above vegetable oils were respectively added into the SrS: Ce NCs suspensions. As shown in Fig. 5 , those pure vegetable oils have little influence on the PL intensities of the SrS: Ce NCs, while the Fe3+ ions involved vegetable oils evidently attenuate the PL intensities of the Ce3+ ions. Compared with the fitting curve shown in Fig. 3b, the calculated Fe3+ ions concentration are 0.47, 0.98, 1.91 and 2.82 μM in the corn oil, peanut oil, colza oil and soybean oil, respectively, which agrees well with the designed concentration.

In summary, monodisperse Ce3+-doped alkaline-earth sulfide NCs are successfully prepared. These blue LED excitable nanoprobes are verified to be suitable for selective and sensitive Fe3+ ions sensing, owing to the NC surface environmental variation greatly influences the radiative probability of the Ce3+: 5d level. The detection limit is measured to be as low as 92.5 nM, which is better than most reported fluorescence probes. Our results open up a new avenue for Fe3+ ions sensing under the excitation of blue LED.

Bingxin Xie, Investigation, Writing-original draft, Visualization. Lei Lei, Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Enyang Liu, Methodology, Visualization. Gongxun Bai, Methodology. Renguang Ye, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Shiqing Xu, Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Provincial Universities of Zhejiang and Science and Technology Innovation Platform and Talent Plan of Zhejiang (2017R52037), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51702306), Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LD18F050001).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2020.117740.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An iron-sulfur domain of the eukaryotic primase is essential for RNA primer synthesis

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In vitro upconverting/downshifting luminescent detection of tumor markers based on Eu3+-activated core-shell-shell lanthanide nanoprobes
